title: People's Liberation Struggle of the Kazakhs Against Colonialism (late 18th – early 20th c.)
overview: Lecture 5 surveys the long anti-colonial resistance of the Kazakhs. Academician M. Kozybaev's periodization counts seven stages from 16th-century resistance to Russian advances in Siberia and the Irtysh to Soviet uprisings against forced collectivization. The Kazakh people raised the banner of freedom more than 400 times. This lecture concentrates on the largest movements: Syrym Datuly (1783–1797), Isatai Taimanuly and Makhambet Utemisuly (1836–1838), Kenesary Kasymuly (1837–1847), and the National Liberation War of 1916.

coreConcepts.definition: Kazakh commoner-peasants – the social base of most 18th–19th c. uprisings. Often impoverished by land seizure and joined nomadic detachments.
coreConcepts.example: Sharua provided most of the 6,200 'sarbaz' (soldiers) under Syrym Datuly by 1783.

coreConcepts.definition: Mass die-off of livestock from severe winter conditions; an economic shock that destabilized the steppe.
coreConcepts.example: The 1782 jute pushed clans into rebellion when the Yaitsk Cossacks compounded the disaster by stealing 4,000 horses.

coreConcepts.definition: A new horde formed by Sultan Bukey in 1801 west of the Urals, between the Volga and Ural rivers; allowed back inside Russia in compensation for the lost steppe.
coreConcepts.example: Site of the 1836–1838 uprising against Khan Zhangir.

coreConcepts.definition: Nicholas II's order to mobilize the non-Russian population of Central Asia and Kazakhstan, ages 18–43, for rear (logistic) work behind the front of WWI.
coreConcepts.example: Immediate catalyst of the National Liberation War of 1916.

coreConcepts.definition: Petition drafted at the Koyandy fair near Karkaraly by the Kazakh bourgeoisie and intelligentsia, addressed to the Russian government.
coreConcepts.example: Demanded suffrage, Kazakh-language office work, jury trials, end to peasant resettlement, freedom for mosque/madrasa construction. Considered the start of the Alash movement.

expandedNotes.heading: Kozybaev's Seven Periods of the National Liberation Movement
expandedNotes.body:
Academician M. Kozybaev (1931–2002) divided Kazakh resistance into 7 stages:
1) Late 16th–early 17th c. – resistance to Russian advance on Siberia and the Irtysh; first khan to oppose was Kuchum of the Siberian Khanate.
2) Early 18th c. – the 'Great Calamity' (Aktaban Shubyryndy); the Patriotic War against the Jungars.
3) Begins 1731 with the acceptance of Russian patronage by part of the Junior Zhuz – colonization continues for nearly two centuries.
4) Apogee of colonial policy; uprisings of Syrym Datuly, Isatai Taimanuly and Makhambet Utemisuly.
5) National liberation movement of Kenesary Kasymov – one of the largest uprisings against Russian imperial policy.
6) Early 20th-century Alash movement, led by the reformist intelligentsia and committed to democratic, market-oriented national autonomy.
7) After October 1917 – more than 370 armed uprisings provoked by forced collectivization, repression of wealthy strata and mass starvation. Each period was a natural continuation of the previous.

expandedNotes.heading: Major Uprisings (full list from the manual)
expandedNotes.body: Largest rebellions: (1) Syrym Datuly 1783–1792; (2) Zholaman Tlenshiuly 1822–1825; (3) Sultan Kaiypgali Yesimuly 1826–1828; (4) Sultan Sarzhan Kasymuly 1824–1836; (5) Isatai Taimanuly and Makhambet Utemisuly 1836–1838; (6) Kenesary Kasymuly 1837–1847; (7) Zhankhozhy Nurmuhamedov 1856–1857; (8) Eset Kotibaruly 1858–1869; (9) Uprising in Mangyshlak 1869–1870; (10) Amangeldy Imanuly 1916.

expandedNotes.heading: Uprising of Syrym Datuly (1783–1797)
expandedNotes.body: Covered the Junior Zhuz from the banks of the Edil (Volga) to the Aral Sea. AIMS: revive Kazakh statehood; return Kazakh lands; restore the traditional nomadic routes; stop Ural Cossack and Khan Nuraly's robbery and violence. TRIGGER: in the winter of 1782 jute killed many herds; the Ural Cossacks then stole 4,000 horses and blocked the Kazakhs from roaming between the Urals and the Volga, sparking a spontaneous revolt. By mid-1783 Syrym fielded 6,200 sarbaz; battles took place near the Orsk fortress. By spring 1785 the rising engulfed the whole Junior Zhuz. Orenburg Governor-General O.A. Igelstrom tried to reform the steppe under the guise of convening a kurultai – he aimed to abolish the khan's power in the Junior Zhuz and divide the Kazakhs into three hordes to weaken them. The uprising continued as partisan war. Internal divisions weakened the movement. In autumn 1797 the tsarist administration installed the puppet Aishuak as new khan; punitive Russian troops intensified pressure; the sultans turned against Syrym; he fled to Khiva and died there in 1803. SIGNIFICANCE: by government decision of 11 March 1801 Kazakhs were allowed to cross to the right bank of the Urals for nomadism – the only uprising of the period that achieved a real concession.

expandedNotes.heading: Uprising of Isatai Taimanuly and Makhambet Utemisuly (1836–1838)
expandedNotes.body: Took place in the Bukey (Inner) Horde between the Volga and Ural rivers and in the southern Caspian region. LEADERS: Isatai Taimanuly (1791–1838), batyr, clan foreman; Makhambet Utemisuly (1804–1840), poet and ideologue. AIMS: revive Kazakh statehood; return Kazakh lands; limit the khan's omnipotence; improve the position of the sharua. EVENTS: started with separate actions against Khan Zhangir; rebels burned noble property and herds. End of October 1837 – 2,000 rebels besieged the khan's residence. November 1837 – major battle in the Tastobe tract; rebels defeated and forced to retreat. Spring 1838 – Isatai and Makhambet regathered forces; defeated decisively in July 1838 in the Akbulak / Kaip area; Isatai killed. Some survivors fled to the Aral Sea region and later joined Kenesary. RESULTS: annual tax increases benefiting the khan and his relatives were halted; tsarist administration began considering Kazakh elders' opinions.

expandedNotes.heading: Uprising of Kenesary Kasymuly (1837–1847)
expandedNotes.body: Lasted ten years, consistently covered all Kazakhstan, and drew broad popular involvement. People sympathized with Kenesary's idea of reviving the united Kazakh Khanate. Researchers call him 'Rebellious Sultan'; Russian researcher Aleksei Levshin called him 'Mithridates of the Kirghiz Steppe'. Kenesary first tried diplomacy – his letters to the Russian Tsar survive in archives; only after diplomatic failure did he rebel. Supported by the Middle, Junior and later Senior Zhuz. Active hostilities began 1837; using partisan tactics (Kazakh weapons were inferior to Russian ones), Kenesary won victories in 1837–1838, including the capture of the Akmola military fortification. In September 1841 he was elected khan; he introduced administrative and judicial reforms and built a disciplined army of 20,000. In July 1844 he destroyed Zhanturin's detachment in the upper Tobol. In 1844 a temporary truce was concluded. By the end of 1846 Russian forces took the territory of the Junior and Middle Zhuzes; Kenesary retreated to the Senior Zhuz. In spring 1847 he died in an internecine war with the Kyrgyz – captured and executed. RESULTS: the ten-year uprising distracted Russian forces and delayed the military conquest of parts of the Middle and Senior Zhuzes and Central Asia. Soviet historian Yermukhan Bekmakhanov was sentenced to 25 years of hard labor for calling Kenesary a national hero rather than a 'class enemy'; American historian Martha Brill Olcott independently called Kenesary 'the first Kazakh nationalist' in a positive sense.

expandedNotes.heading: Smaller Uprisings of Mid-19th Century
expandedNotes.body: Iset Kotibaruly's uprising 1853–1857; Zhankhozhy Nurmuhamedov's uprising 1856–1858; uprising in Mangyshlak 1869–1870. None matched Kenesary's scale, but they kept the resistance tradition alive.

expandedNotes.heading: Alash Movement Origins (1905–1914)
expandedNotes.body: By the late 19th century the colonial seizure of Kazakh land was complete and a new actor emerged: the Kazakh intelligentsia (Chokan Valikhanov, Abai Kunanbaev, Ybyray Altynsarin and later Shormanov, Kazangapov, Kosshygulov). The bourgeoisie and intelligentsia took the lead. Main forms of struggle: PETITIONS – the Karkaraly Petition of 25 July 1905 at the Koyandy fair, demanding: stop peasant resettlement; separate spiritual administration; abolition of censorship; Kazakh-language office work; jury trials; Kazakh deputies in the State Duma. DUMA PARTICIPATION – 9 Kazakhs served as deputies: 1st Duma (1906) – Birimzhanov, Bukeikhanov, Koshulov, Kalmenov, Zhantorin, Kulmanov, Tundutov, Tayynov; 2nd Duma (1907) – Allabergenov, Birimzhanov, Koshulov, Kulmanov, Nurekenov, Tynyshpaev, Karataev. Deputy B. Karataev addressed the 2nd Duma denouncing the colonial agrarian policy. The 1st and 2nd Dumas were dissolved within months and Kazakhs lost the franchise. PRESS – 1911–1915 magazine 'Aiqap' (Mukhamedzhan Seralin, Zhihansha Seydalin, Yerkem Alimuly); 1913–1916 newspaper 'Qazaq' in Orenburg edited by Ahmet Baitursynuly – the platform of future Alash leaders. Repressions only consolidated the movement; 1905–1914 prepared the emergence of the Alash political party.

expandedNotes.heading: National Liberation War of 1916
expandedNotes.body: Cause: Nicholas II's decree of 25 June 1916 ordering mobilization for rear work of 'aliens' (inorodtsy) aged 18 to 43 – the main breadwinners of Kazakh families. Two military centers formed: TURGAI (leader: batyr Amangeldy Imanuly; political organizer: Alibi Zhangildin) and ZHETYSU/SEMIRECHYE (leaders: batyrs Tokash Bokin, Bekbolat Ashekeyev, Uzak Saurykov, Zhemenken Mambetov). MANAGEMENT: rebels elected khans, sardarbeks (military leaders), a council of khans and biys, and treasurers; organized armed forces. TURGAI: center at the Batpakkara tract; about 50,000 rebels. On 22 October 1916 they besieged Turgay. Unequal forces and punitive Russian expeditions forced a transition to guerrilla warfare; the uprising continued into 1917 and merged with the events of the February Revolution. ZHETYSU: rebels were defeated; more than 400,000 Kazakhs were forced to flee abroad (mainly to China). POSITIONS OF THE INTELLIGENTSIA: divided – moderates (Baitursynov, Bukeikhanov, Tynyshpaev, Dulatov, Sh. Qudaiberdiuly) believed in negotiation and feared mass casualties; radicals (T. Ryskulov, S. Seyfullin, A. Zhangaeldin, S. Mendeshev) supported the rising. Newspaper 'Qazaq' covered the events extensively. OUTCOMES: brutal suppression; thousands sent to prison or penal servitude; mass migration to China; the rising influenced the political situation and became a precondition for the post-February 1917 political map of Central Asia.

keyScholars.name: Syrym Datuly
keyScholars.contribution: Batyr and foreman of the Baibakty clan; led the first major anti-Russian uprising in the Junior Zhuz (1783–1797).

keyScholars.name: Isatai Taimanuly
keyScholars.contribution: Batyr and clan foreman; military leader of the 1836–1838 uprising in the Bukey Horde.

keyScholars.name: Makhambet Utemisuly
keyScholars.contribution: Warrior-poet and ideologue of the 1836–1838 uprising; his verses are a manifesto of Kazakh freedom.

keyScholars.name: Kenesary Kasymuly
keyScholars.contribution: Grandson of Abylai; last all-Kazakh khan (elected 1841); led the largest anti-colonial uprising (1837–1847).

keyScholars.name: Amangeldy Imanuly
keyScholars.contribution: Batyr; commander of the Turgay center of the 1916 uprising.

keyScholars.name: Alibi Zhangildin
keyScholars.contribution: Political organizer of the Turgay 1916 uprising; later prominent Soviet Kazakh figure.

keyScholars.name: Tokash Bokin
keyScholars.contribution: Batyr; leader of the Zhetysu/Semirechye center of the 1916 uprising.

keyScholars.name: Yermukhan Bekmakhanov
keyScholars.contribution: Historian; author of 'Kazakhstan in the 20–40s of the 19th c.'; sentenced to 25 years of hard labor for treating Kenesary as a national hero.

keyScholars.name: M. Kozybaev
keyScholars.contribution: Academician; created the standard 7-period scheme of the Kazakh national liberation movement.

keyScholars.name: Aleksei Levshin
keyScholars.contribution: Russian researcher; coined 'Mithridates of the Kirghiz Steppe' for Kenesary.

keyScholars.name: Martha Brill Olcott
keyScholars.contribution: American historian; described Kenesary as 'the first Kazakh nationalist' in a positive sense, supporting Bekmakhanov's reading.

keyScholars.name: Ahmet Baitursynuly
keyScholars.contribution: Editor of 'Qazaq' (1913–1918); future leader of Alash.

keyScholars.name: Bakhytzhan Karataev
keyScholars.contribution: Kazakh deputy in the 2nd State Duma; denounced colonial agrarian policy from the Duma rostrum.

comparativeTables.title: Three Major 18th–19th-Century Uprisings
rows: Right to roam between Urals and Volga (1801)
Halted khan's tax rises; elders consulted

comparativeTables.title: 1916 Uprising – Two Centers
rows: Most successful; 50,000 rebels; siege of Turgay 22 Oct 1916; guerrilla war into 1917
Tokash Bokin, Bekbolat Ashekeyev, Uzak Saurykov, Zhemenken Mambetov

comparativeTables.title: Kazakh Deputies in the State Duma
rows: 8 (Birimzhanov, Bukeikhanov, Koshulov, Kalmenov, Zhantorin, Kulmanov, Tundutov, Tayynov)
7 (Allabergenov, Birimzhanov, Koshulov, Kulmanov, Nurekenov, Tynyshpaev, Karataev)

realWorldExamples: Statues of Isatai and Makhambet ride together in Atyrau – the western Kazakh symbol of resistance.
Kenesary Kasymuly's equestrian monument stands in central Astana on the embankment.
Amangeldy Imanuly's monument is in his home region; his name was given to a town in Kostanai oblast.
Makhambet's verses ('My word is a sharp sword…') are part of the standard Kazakh literature curriculum.
The newspaper 'Qazaq' (1913–1918) is preserved and digitized as a foundational document of national consciousness.
More than 400,000 Kazakhs fled to Xinjiang in 1916 – their descendants are the basis of the modern Kazakh diaspora in China.

timeline.event: Winter jute; Ural Cossacks steal 4,000 horses, block Kazakh nomadic routes
Igelstrom reform attempts to abolish the khan's power in the Junior Zhuz
Kazakhs allowed to cross to right bank of the Urals (Bukey/Inner Horde formed)
Uprising of Isatai Taimanuly and Makhambet Utemisuly; Isatai killed July 1838
Kenesary destroys Zhanturin's detachment in the upper Tobol
Kenesary killed in war with the Kyrgyz
Karkaraly Petition – birth of the Alash movement
First Russian State Duma – 8 Kazakh deputies
Second State Duma – 7 Kazakh deputies; B. Karataev's speech
Decree on requisition of inorodtsy for rear work
Uprising begins in Semirechye and Turgai

mustMemorize: Kozybaev's periodization: 7 stages of the national liberation movement
Syrym Datuly 1783–1797: Junior Zhuz; result – right to roam between Urals and Volga (1801)
Isatai Taimanuly & Makhambet Utemisuly 1836–1838: Bukey Horde, against Khan Zhangir; Isatai died July 1838 in Akbulak
Kenesary Kasymuly 1837–1847: all three zhuzes; elected khan Sept 1841; killed by Kyrgyz 1847; 'Mithridates of the Kirghiz Steppe' (Levshin)
25 July 1905 – Karkaraly Petition (Koyandy fair) – beginning of Alash
Decree 25 June 1916 → 1916 uprising; Turgai (Amangeldy Imanuly) succeeds, Zhetysu defeated; 400,000+ flee to China
cheatSheet:
Syrym (1783) → Isatai+Makhambet (1836) → Kenesary (1837, khan 1841) → 1905 Karkaraly petition → 1906/1907 Duma → 1913 Qazaq → 1916 uprising (Amangeldy Turgai vs Zhetysu defeat)
Kozybaev = 7 periods
Aims of all: revive statehood, return lands, limit khan/colonial power, help sharua
mnemonics: S-I-K-A: Syrym, Isatai, Kenesary, Amangeldy – the 4 main leaders in chronological order
25/6/16 – the decree that exploded the steppe
Karkaraly = 'KArt with KARta' – petition that put the Kazakhs on the political map (1905)
